clinicalStem: A 62-year-old hyperopic woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-hour history of severe right-sided headache, nausea, vomiting, and blurred vision. She noticed halos around lights while watching television. On examination, the right eye is red with a hazy cornea, the pupil is mid-dilated and fixed, and visual acuity is markedly reduced. Intraocular pressure (IOP) measured by tonometry is 52 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma classically presents with sudden severe eye pain, headache, nausea, vomiting, halos around lights, a hazy (steamy) cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, and markedly elevated IOP — all present here. Hyperopia is a known risk factor because a shallower anterior chamber predisposes to angle closure. The IOP of 52 mmHg (normal <21 mmHg) is diagnostic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: the triad of fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, and IOP 52 mmHg with acute pain/halos is pathognomonic of acute angle-closure glaucoma.
Anterior uveitis causes a small, irregular (not mid-dilated fixed) pupil, ciliary flush, and keratic precipitates; IOP may be low or mildly elevated, not acutely 52 mmHg.
Corneal ulcer causes pain and a white corneal opacity but does not produce a fixed mid-dilated pupil or IOP of 52 mmHg.
Primary open-angle glaucoma is insidious and painless; it does not cause acute headache, nausea, or a hazy cornea.
Migraine aura causes visual disturbance but does not produce a fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, or elevated IOP measured on tonometry.
clinicalPearl: Mid-dilated fixed pupil + hazy cornea + IOP >40 mmHg + halos = acute angle-closure glaucoma until proven otherwise — an ophthalmic emergency.
keyClue: Fixed mid-dilated pupil, hazy cornea, IOP 52 mmHg, halos around lights in a hyperopic woman
commonExamTrap: Attributing the headache and vomiting to migraine and missing the ocular signs that point to acute angle-closure glaucoma.
missedConcept: Acute angle-closure glaucoma must be distinguished from open-angle glaucoma: the former is a painful emergency with a dramatically elevated IOP, while the latter is a silent, chronic disease.
redFlagReason: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic emergency — untreated, IOP >40 mmHg causes irreversible optic nerve damage and permanent blindness within hours.

subtopic: Optic disc and visual field assessment
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a family history of glaucoma attends a routine optometry appointment. He has no symptoms. Visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally. IOP is 26 mmHg in both eyes. Slit-lamp examination reveals a cup-to-disc ratio of 0.8 bilaterally with superior notching of the neuroretinal rim. Gonioscopy shows wide-open angles. Which single investigation is most important to confirm the extent of functional damage?
options: Optical coherence tomography of the optic nerve head
explanation: Automated perimetry (Humphrey visual field testing) directly measures functional loss — the arcuate scotoma and nasal step defects characteristic of primary open-angle glaucoma. Structural damage (enlarged cup, rim notching) must be correlated with functional loss to stage disease and guide treatment. Perimetry is the standard test to map glaucomatous field defects.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: automated perimetry maps functional visual field loss (arcuate scotomas, nasal step) that confirms glaucomatous damage and guides management decisions.

OCT of the optic nerve/RNFL quantifies structural thinning and is complementary, but the question asks for the investigation most important to confirm functional damage — which is visual field testing.
Ultrasound biomicroscopy images the anterior segment for angle anatomy; it is used in angle-closure assessment, not functional glaucoma staging.
Corneal pachymetry measures central corneal thickness to correct IOP readings; it does not assess functional visual field loss.
Fluorescein angiography images retinal vasculature and is used in retinal vascular disease, not glaucoma field assessment.
clinicalPearl: Glaucoma is diagnosed structurally (cup:disc ratio, rim notching) but staged and followed functionally with automated perimetry — field loss is the endpoint that matters to the patient.
keyClue: Cup-to-disc ratio 0.8 with superior notching and elevated IOP — functional loss must be mapped with visual field testing
commonExamTrap: Choosing OCT instead of perimetry; OCT is structural, not the primary test for functional (field) damage.
missedConcept: Open-angle glaucoma causes silent, progressive visual field loss; perimetry is required to detect and monitor this functional damage.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old man with a known history of ankylosing spondylitis presents with a 2-day history of acute-onset left eye pain, photophobia, and blurred vision. He denies any discharge. On examination, the left eye shows circumcorneal (ciliary) flush. Slit-lamp examination reveals fine keratic precipitates on the inferior corneal endothelium, cells and flare in the anterior chamber, and a small, irregular pupil. IOP is 14 mmHg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Anterior uveitis (iritis) presents with pain, photophobia, ciliary flush, anterior chamber cells and flare, keratic precipitates, and a small irregular pupil due to posterior synechiae — all present here. The association with HLA-B27 conditions such as ankylosing spondylitis is classic. Normal IOP distinguishes this from acute glaucoma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: anterior uveitis in the context of ankylosing spondylitis is the classic presentation — ciliary flush, AC cells/flare, keratic precipitates, and a small irregular pupil.
Acute angle-closure glaucoma produces a mid-dilated fixed pupil, hazy cornea, and IOP >40 mmHg — not the small irregular pupil and normal IOP seen here.
Bacterial conjunctivitis causes mucopurulent discharge, papillary reaction, and diffuse conjunctival injection without anterior chamber inflammation or keratic precipitates.
Posterior uveitis (choroiditis) presents with floaters and visual loss without pain, photophobia, or anterior chamber cells; it does not cause ciliary flush.
Viral keratitis (HSV) causes a dendritic corneal ulcer and reduced corneal sensation; it does not cause anterior chamber cells, flare, or keratic precipitates in the classical presentation.
clinicalPearl: In a young HLA-B27-associated disease patient with acute painful red eye, photophobia, ciliary flush, and small irregular pupil — diagnose anterior uveitis and treat promptly to prevent synechiae and secondary glaucoma.
keyClue: Ankylosing spondylitis + ciliary flush + anterior chamber cells/flare + small irregular pupil
commonExamTrap: Confusing anterior uveitis with acute angle-closure glaucoma; the key differentiator is a small (not mid-dilated) irregular pupil and normal IOP in uveitis.
missedConcept: Anterior uveitis is strongly associated with HLA-B27 conditions (AS, Reiter syndrome, psoriatic arthritis, IBD) and requires slit-lamp examination for definitive diagnosis.

subtopic: Nuclear sclerotic cataract in the elderly
clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 1-year history of gradually worsening bilateral blurred vision. She reports difficulty reading small print and driving at night due to glare from oncoming headlights. She also mentions needing brighter light to read. Visual acuity is 6/18 in both eyes and does not improve with a pinhole test. Fundoscopy is difficult to perform because of poor red reflex. Slit-lamp examination shows bilateral yellow-brown lens opacity densest at the nucleus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Nuclear sclerotic cataract, the most common type in the elderly, presents with gradual painless visual deterioration, increased glare, poor night vision, and difficulty with tasks requiring fine detail. The characteristic finding is a yellow-brown dense opacity at the lens nucleus on slit-lamp examination and a diminished or absent red reflex. Vision does not improve with a pinhole because the problem is within the lens, not a refractive error.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: gradual painless vision loss + yellow-brown nuclear lens opacity + reduced red reflex + no pinhole improvement in an elderly patient = nuclear sclerotic cataract.

Posterior subcapsular cataract also causes glare and near-vision difficulty but occurs earlier in life, is associated with steroid use or diabetes, and the opacity is at the posterior pole of the lens — not the nucleus.
Age-related macular degeneration causes central vision loss and distortion (metamorphopsia) but does not produce a dense lens opacity or reduced red reflex.
Primary open-angle glaucoma is painless with peripheral field loss but preserves central acuity until late; it does not cause lens opacity or reduced red reflex.
Diabetic retinopathy affects the retina and may reduce vision, but slit-lamp examination would show a clear lens, not a nuclear opacity with reduced red reflex.
clinicalPearl: Cataract is the leading cause of reversible blindness worldwide; nuclear sclerotic cataract presents with gradual glare, reduced red reflex, and a yellow-brown nuclear lens opacity on slit-lamp.
keyClue: Yellow-brown nuclear lens opacity on slit-lamp with diminished red reflex in an elderly woman
commonExamTrap: Ordering retinal investigations before confirming a cataract with slit-lamp; always examine the lens when fundoscopy is difficult due to poor red reflex.
missedConcept: No pinhole improvement distinguishes a lenticular (cataract) cause from an uncorrected refractive error — both reduce vision, but only refractive error improves with pinhole.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to A&E with a 2-day history of left eyelid swelling following a recent episode of sinusitis. On examination he has left periorbital oedema with erythema extending to the upper and lower eyelids. He is pyrexial (38.9 °C). On closer examination there is resistance to retropulsion of the globe, 4 mm of proptosis, restricted extra-ocular movements in all directions, and reduced visual acuity of 6/24 in the left eye. He appears unwell and in pain. Which investigation is most urgently required to guide management?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT scan of the orbits and paranasal sinuses
MRI of the orbit with gadolinium
explanation: The combination of proptosis, restricted extra-ocular movements, and reduced visual acuity distinguishes true orbital (post-septal) cellulitis from pre-septal cellulitis. This is a sight-threatening and potentially life-threatening emergency. Contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and sinuses is the investigation of choice: it rapidly confirms orbital involvement, identifies a subperiosteal or orbital abscess requiring surgical drainage, and reveals the sinusitis source — information critical for immediate surgical and antibiotic decision-making.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: contrast CT orbits/sinuses is the gold-standard urgent investigation to delineate orbital abscess, confirm post-septal involvement, and direct surgical versus medical management.
Plain X-ray of the sinuses may show sinusitis but cannot image soft-tissue orbital contents, cannot identify an abscess, and will not change management in a child with post-septal signs.
Orbital ultrasound is limited for posterior orbital assessment and is operator-dependent; it cannot reliably exclude a deep subperiosteal abscess or assess intracranial extension.
Blood cultures are important but alone do not guide whether surgical drainage of an orbital abscess is needed — structural imaging is the priority.

MRI provides superior soft-tissue detail and is ideal once the patient is stable, but it is slower to acquire than CT and not the first-line choice in an acute emergency requiring rapid surgical decision-making.
clinicalPearl: Proptosis + restricted EOM + reduced vision = post-septal (orbital) cellulitis until CT proves otherwise — urgent contrast CT is mandatory to exclude subperiosteal abscess requiring surgical drainage.
keyClue: Proptosis, restricted eye movements, and reduced visual acuity distinguishing orbital (post-septal) from pre-septal cellulitis
commonExamTrap: Treating a child with proptosis and restricted EOM as pre-septal cellulitis with oral antibiotics alone without imaging — missing an orbital abscess.
missedConcept: Pre-septal cellulitis is anterior to the orbital septum and does not cause proptosis or restricted movement; any sign of post-septal involvement mandates urgent CT and possible surgery.
redFlagReason: Orbital cellulitis with abscess can cause optic nerve compression (blindness), cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, and brain abscess — an ophthalmic and neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent CT and IV antibiotics.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of pain, swelling, and erythema over the medial canthus of her right eye. She reports a 6-month history of epiphora (excessive tearing) in that eye. On examination there is a tender, fluctuant swelling over the right lacrimal sac area (below the medial canthal tendon). Gentle pressure over the swelling produces mucopurulent discharge through the right lower punctum. She is afebrile and systemically well. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Acute dacryocystitis is infection of the lacrimal sac, characteristically presenting with tender swelling below the medial canthal tendon (over the lacrimal sac fossa), erythema, and — pathognomonically — regurgitation of mucopurulent material through the punctum on pressure. A prior history of epiphora indicates underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction, which causes stasis and secondary infection. This is distinct from dacryoadenitis (lacrimal gland, superior-lateral orbit) and from orbital cellulitis (proptosis, restricted EOM).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: fluctuant medial canthal swelling below the canthal tendon with mucopurulent reflux through the punctum on pressure = acute dacryocystitis arising from nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
Ethmoidal sinusitis causes medial orbital wall pain and nasal symptoms; it does not produce a palpable lacrimal sac swelling with mucopurulent discharge through the punctum.
A lacrimal gland tumour presents as a painless or slowly enlarging mass at the superolateral orbit (not medial canthus) without discharge through the punctum.
Orbital cellulitis causes proptosis, restricted extra-ocular movements, and vision change; it does not produce localised lacrimal sac swelling with punctal reflux.
Dacryoadenitis is inflammation of the lacrimal gland at the superolateral orbit with S-shaped ptosis; it does not cause medial canthal swelling or mucopurulent punctal reflux.
clinicalPearl: Mucopurulent discharge through the lower punctum on pressure over the medial canthal region is pathognomonic of dacryocystitis — the preceding epiphora signals the underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
keyClue: Mucopurulent reflux through the punctum on pressure over the lacrimal sac area in a patient with pre-existing epiphora
commonExamTrap: Mistaking the medial canthal swelling of dacryocystitis for a dental or sinus abscess; the key is reflux through the punctum.
missedConcept: Dacryocystitis almost always has an underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction; definitive treatment after acute resolution is dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR).

subtopic: Horner syndrome vs third nerve palsy
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with a 2-week history of right-sided drooping of the eyelid. On examination he has 2 mm of right ptosis and right miosis (pupil 2 mm right vs 4 mm left), with normal extra-ocular movements and no diplopia. The anhidrosis is noted over the right side of the face. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and reports a new persistent dry cough. Which investigation is most important to identify the underlying cause?
options: CT chest (imaging for apical lung tumour)
explanation: This man has a Horner syndrome (partial ptosis + ipsilateral miosis + anhidrosis) — not a third nerve palsy (which causes complete ptosis, mydriasis, and ophthalmoplegia). In a long-term smoker with a new cough, Horner syndrome at the first neuron level is a classic presentation of a Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus lung carcinoma) invading the cervical sympathetic chain. CT chest to identify an apical lung mass is the most urgent and important investigation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Horner syndrome (partial ptosis + miosis) in a heavy smoker with new cough demands urgent CT chest to exclude a Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus carcinoma) compressing the cervical sympathetic chain.

MRI brain is appropriate if a central cause (hypothalamus, brainstem) of Horner syndrome is suspected, but the peripheral localization clues (anhidrosis, smoking history, cough) make a chest cause far more likely as the first investigation.
The Edrophonium (Tensilon) test diagnoses myasthenia gravis, which causes bilateral fatigable ptosis without miosis or anhidrosis — not the unilateral partial ptosis with miosis seen here.
Thyroid function tests are relevant for thyroid eye disease (lid retraction, proptosis) but do not explain miosis and anhidrosis.
Miosis from opioid use is bilateral, not unilateral, and would not be associated with unilateral ptosis and ipsilateral anhidrosis.
clinicalPearl: Horner syndrome = partial ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis; in a smoker with new cough this triad mandates urgent CT chest to exclude Pancoast tumour.
keyClue: Partial ptosis + miosis (not mydriasis) + ipsilateral facial anhidrosis + heavy smoker with new cough = Horner syndrome from apical lung mass
commonExamTrap: Confusing Horner syndrome (miosis, partial ptosis) with third nerve palsy (mydriasis, complete ptosis, ophthalmoplegia); the pupil size is the critical differentiator.
missedConcept: Horner syndrome causes partial ptosis and MIOSIS — the opposite of third nerve palsy which causes complete ptosis and MYDRIASIS. Anhidrosis localises the lesion.
redFlagReason: Horner syndrome in a smoker with respiratory symptoms may represent a Pancoast (apical lung) tumour — a malignancy that is missed if the neurological finding is not investigated urgently.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old myopic man presents with a sudden-onset shower of black floaters in his right eye followed by a dark shadow encroaching from the inferior periphery of his visual field over the preceding 48 hours. Visual acuity is 6/9 in the affected eye. On fundoscopy a grey, undulating retina is visible in the superior field with a visible horseshoe tear at the 11 o'clock position. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Posterior vitreous detachment without retinal tear
explanation: Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment results from a full-thickness retinal break (here a horseshoe tear) allowing liquefied vitreous to pass beneath the retina and separate it from the RPE. The classic prodrome is a sudden shower of floaters (vitreous detachment), followed by a curtain or shadow in the visual field as the retina detaches. Myopia is the most important risk factor. The visible grey undulating retina with a horseshoe tear on fundoscopy is diagnostic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: horseshoe tear + grey undulating retina + field shadow in a myope with floaters = rhegmatogenous retinal detachment — a surgical emergency.
Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) alone causes acute floaters and photopsia but does not cause a field shadow or visible retinal elevation — fundoscopy is normal in uncomplicated PVD.
Vitreous haemorrhage causes a sudden loss of red reflex and reduced vision but fundoscopy shows a blocked view, not a visible grey detached retina.
Central retinal artery occlusion causes sudden painless profound visual loss with a pale retina and cherry-red spot; it does not produce floaters or a peripheral grey undulating retina.
Choroidal melanoma may cause secondary retinal detachment but typically appears as an elevated pigmented fundal mass, not a peripheral horseshoe tear with grey undulating retina.
clinicalPearl: Floaters + photopsia + progressive field shadow in a myope = rhegmatogenous retinal detachment until proven otherwise — urgent same-day ophthalmology review is mandatory.
keyClue: Grey undulating retina with a horseshoe tear visible on fundoscopy + peripheral field shadow + acute floaters in a myope
commonExamTrap: Reassuring the patient with floaters as a benign PVD without fundoscopy — missing the retinal tear or early detachment that requires urgent repair.
missedConcept: All patients with acute floaters must have a dilated fundal examination to exclude a retinal tear; an undetected tear progresses to full detachment.
redFlagReason: Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment involving or threatening the macula is a surgical emergency — delay beyond 24 hours of macular involvement causes irreversible central vision loss.

topic: Central retinal vein occlusion — diagnosis and investigation
clinicalStem: A 68-year-old hypertensive man with type 2 diabetes presents with painless blurred vision in his right eye that developed over one morning. Visual acuity is 6/60 in the right eye (6/9 left). On fundoscopy of the right eye there are extensive flame-shaped haemorrhages in all four quadrants, dilated tortuous retinal veins, disc swelling, and cotton-wool spots in the distribution of all four quadrant vessels. The left fundus is normal. Which investigation is most important to identify the underlying systemic risk factor contributing to this presentation?
options: Fasting blood glucose and HbA1c with blood pressure measurement
Optical coherence tomography of the macula
explanation: The fundal picture — flame haemorrhages in all four quadrants, dilated tortuous veins, disc swelling, and cotton-wool spots — is the classic 'blood and thunder' appearance of central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO). The most important systemic risk factors are hypertension and diabetes mellitus. In a known hypertensive diabetic the most clinically relevant investigation to quantify modifiable risk (and guide systemic treatment to prevent fellow-eye involvement) is blood pressure measurement plus fasting glucose/HbA1c.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: blood pressure and glycaemic control are the primary modifiable systemic risk factors in CRVO; identifying and treating them reduces the risk of fellow-eye occlusion and systemic vascular events.

Fluorescein angiography delineates areas of capillary non-perfusion and neovascularisation — important for planning laser treatment — but does not identify the underlying systemic aetiology asked for in this question.
OCT of the macula quantifies macular oedema and guides intravitreal injection therapy, but it is a structural retinal investigation, not a systemic risk-factor screen.
Visual evoked potentials assess optic nerve conduction and are used in demyelinating disease; they are not relevant in the investigation of CRVO.

Carotid Doppler is relevant in transient ischaemic attack and amaurosis fugax (retinal emboli from carotid atherosclerosis); CRVO is a venous — not embolic arterial — occlusion, making carotid imaging a lower-priority systemic investigation.
clinicalPearl: CRVO's blood-and-thunder fundus (flame haemorrhages in all 4 quadrants, dilated tortuous veins, disc swelling) demands urgent blood pressure control and glycaemic optimisation — the most important modifiable systemic risk factors.
keyClue: Flame haemorrhages in all four quadrants with dilated tortuous veins and disc swelling = CRVO; hypertension and diabetes are the primary systemic culprits
commonExamTrap: Ordering fluorescein angiography or OCT first when the question asks for the systemic investigation — the systemic cause (hypertension/diabetes) is the priority question here.
missedConcept: CRVO is associated with hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidaemia, and hyperviscosity states; systemic investigation and risk-factor control are as important as the ophthalmic treatment.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old man with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes (HbA1c 11.2%) attends diabetic eye screening. He has no visual symptoms. On dilated fundoscopy both eyes show multiple dot and blot haemorrhages, hard exudates, microaneurysms, and venous beading. New vessels are also visible at the disc (NVD) in the right eye and in the periphery (NVE) of the left eye. Which stage of diabetic retinopathy is present?
explanation: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) is defined by the presence of new vessels — neovascularisation of the disc (NVD) or periphery (NVE) — representing pathological angiogenesis driven by retinal ischaemia. Background features (microaneurysms, haemorrhages, exudates) are present as a substrate, but the hallmark diagnostic criterion for proliferative disease is the new vessel growth, which is present here in both eyes. PDR carries a high risk of vitreous haemorrhage and traction retinal detachment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: the presence of new vessels at the disc (NVD) and at the periphery (NVE) defines proliferative diabetic retinopathy regardless of background changes.
Mild NPDR shows microaneurysms only — without haemorrhages, exudates, venous changes, or new vessels.
Moderate NPDR has haemorrhages, hard exudates, and microaneurysms in fewer than all four quadrants but no new vessels.

Severe NPDR (4-2-1 rule) has >20 haemorrhages in 4 quadrants, venous beading in 2 quadrants, or IRMA in 1 quadrant — but by definition has NO new vessels; once new vessels appear, it is classified as proliferative.
Diabetic macular oedema is graded separately and can occur at any stage of DR; it does not account for the new vessels seen here.
clinicalPearl: New vessels (NVD or NVE) = proliferative diabetic retinopathy — no matter what background changes coexist; PDR requires urgent pan-retinal photocoagulation or anti-VEGF therapy.
keyClue: New vessels at the disc (NVD) and in the periphery (NVE) — the defining feature of proliferative diabetic retinopathy
commonExamTrap: Staging as severe NPDR because of venous beading and multiple haemorrhages, while missing that the presence of new vessels automatically upgrades to PDR.
missedConcept: The single most important finding differentiating NPDR from PDR is the presence of any neovascularisation; once new vessels appear, the classification changes and treatment urgency escalates.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis presents with a 6-month history of bilateral burning, gritty, and sandy sensation in both eyes, worsening throughout the day and with prolonged reading or screen use. She reports temporary relief with artificial tear drops. Schirmer's test shows wetting of only 4 mm in 5 minutes bilaterally (normal >10 mm). Slit-lamp examination with rose Bengal staining shows punctate staining of the inferior cornea and conjunctiva. Tear break-up time (TBUT) is 5 seconds bilaterally (normal >10 seconds). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Dry eye disease (keratoconjunctivitis sicca) is diagnosed by the combination of symptoms (gritty burning, worse with prolonged use, relieved by drops) plus objective signs: Schirmer's test <10 mm/5 min (aqueous deficiency), TBUT <10 seconds (evaporative dysfunction), and rose Bengal/fluorescein staining of the ocular surface epithelium. The association with rheumatoid arthritis points to secondary Sjogren's syndrome as the aetiology.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Schirmer's 4 mm + TBUT 5 seconds + punctate rose Bengal staining in a patient with RA = dry eye disease (keratoconjunctivitis sicca), likely secondary Sjogren's syndrome.
Allergic conjunctivitis causes itching (not grittiness), papillary response, and seasonal/contact triggers; Schirmer's and TBUT are normal and rose Bengal staining is absent.
Bacterial conjunctivitis causes mucopurulent discharge, morning crusting, and conjunctival injection; it does not produce reduced Schirmer's or shortened TBUT.

Viral keratoconjunctivitis (adenoviral) presents acutely with watery discharge, follicles, preauricular lymphadenopathy, and corneal subepithelial infiltrates — not chronic gritty burning with reduced TBUT.

Blepharitis causes lid margin inflammation with crusting and meibomian gland dysfunction; it can contribute to evaporative dry eye but alone does not explain Schirmer's of 4 mm indicating aqueous tear deficiency.
clinicalPearl: Dry eye diagnosis requires both symptoms AND objective tests: Schirmer's <10 mm/5 min indicates aqueous deficiency; TBUT <10 seconds indicates evaporative dysfunction; rose Bengal staining marks epithelial damage.
keyClue: Schirmer's 4 mm + TBUT 5 seconds + rose Bengal punctate staining + bilateral gritty burning in a patient with rheumatoid arthritis
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing allergic conjunctivitis because of bilateral eye symptoms; the absence of itching, the abnormal Schirmer's, and the association with RA all point to dry eye disease.
missedConcept: Dry eye disease in rheumatoid arthritis or other connective tissue disorders suggests secondary Sjogren's syndrome — systemic investigation (anti-Ro/La antibodies) may be warranted.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old obese woman presents with a 3-week history of daily headaches that are worse on waking and on bending forward, associated with transient visual obscurations lasting seconds. Visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally. Colour vision is intact. On fundoscopy there is bilateral disc swelling with blurred disc margins, absent venous pulsations, and C-shaped haemorrhages around both optic discs. Visual field testing shows enlarged blind spots bilaterally. Which investigation should be performed first?
options: Neuroimaging (CT or MRI brain) before lumbar puncture
Lumbar puncture to measure opening CSF pressure
Optical coherence tomography of the optic nerve
Formal visual field testing with automated perimetry
Orbital ultrasound for optic nerve sheath diameter
explanation: The fundoscopic picture of bilateral disc swelling with peripapillary haemorrhages, absent venous pulsations, and enlarged blind spots indicates true papilloedema from raised intracranial pressure (ICP). Before lumbar puncture (LP) is performed — which is required to confirm raised ICP and diagnose idiopathic intracranial hypertension — neuroimaging must exclude a space-occupying lesion or cerebral venous thrombosis, because LP in the presence of raised ICP from a mass lesion risks fatal transtentorial herniation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: neuroimaging (CT/MRI brain) must precede lumbar puncture in all cases of suspected raised ICP with papilloedema to exclude a mass lesion before LP — performing LP first risks fatal coning.
Lumbar puncture is the definitive test for measuring opening CSF pressure and confirming IIH, but it is contraindicated until neuroimaging excludes a space-occupying lesion causing raised ICP.
OCT of the optic nerve quantifies disc swelling and RNFL thickness and can monitor progression, but it does not identify the cause of raised ICP and does not replace neuroimaging.
Formal visual field testing is important to monitor visual function in papilloedema but does not identify the intracranial cause and should not delay urgent neuroimaging.
Orbital ultrasound measuring optic nerve sheath diameter >5 mm is a bedside surrogate for raised ICP, but it is not a substitute for CT/MRI that can identify a mass lesion before LP.
clinicalPearl: Neuroimaging BEFORE lumbar puncture is a non-negotiable safety rule in suspected raised ICP — LP without prior imaging in a mass-effect lesion risks transtentorial herniation and death.
keyClue: Bilateral disc swelling with absent venous pulsations and enlarged blind spots = papilloedema; neuroimaging must precede LP to exclude a mass before CSF drainage
commonExamTrap: Rushing to perform lumbar puncture to measure CSF pressure before excluding a space-occupying lesion — the classic fatal error in papilloedema management.
missedConcept: Papilloedema from raised ICP has many causes (idiopathic IIH, cerebral venous thrombosis, SOL); neuroimaging is mandatory before LP to safely exclude herniation risk.
redFlagReason: Papilloedema indicates raised intracranial pressure; lumbar puncture without prior neuroimaging to exclude a mass lesion can precipitate fatal transtentorial (uncal) herniation.
